quote: We we had it from it's been about two weeks. I think it's, March 18, but we tried a a request from you for the $1.03 2 in Mammoth Street in Portsmouth.
stewart_read: Parker uses specific address and date to legitimize the call after Vincent's 'I don't remember' challenge. This is a positive coaching moment — specificity reduces suspicion, and Parker deploys it instinctively. The codex's how_did_you_get_my_number protocol calls for exactly this: 'Specificity reduces suspicion. Vagueness increases it.' Parker executes the spirit of the protocol without being prompted.
coaching_implication: Reinforce this move explicitly. Parker may not know he did something right here. Manager should name it: 'When a customer says they don't remember the request, you anchored with the address and date — that's the correct move and it kept Vincent on the line. Do that every time.'

stewart_read: Vincent names the installation fee as the specific reason he lost interest. This is the highest-leverage flip opportunity in the call. Parker responds with a feature-level reframe ('we don't actually have anything upfront') rather than an emotional inversion. The codex's objection_inversion move was directly available: the trigger 'I can't afford it' maps to the flip 'What you can't afford is your current bill.' Parker's response moves Vincent off resistance but leaves the emotional alignment-against-the-utility on the table entirely.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Parker may have deliberately kept the reframe light to avoid over-selling on a recycled lead, preserving the closer's room to build the full financial case. Rejected — there is no evidence of a deliberate setup; Parker's response reads as the first available safe answer, not a planned deferral.
coaching_implication: This is the call's primary flip miss. Manager should walk Parker through the inversion sequence: (1) acknowledge the fee concern, (2) redirect to the current bill as the ongoing cost, (3) frame the appointment as the place where the math becomes visible. Suggested intent (not verbatim): 'The fee you're worried about is a one-time thing — the bill you're paying right now never stops. That's the cost worth looking at. Let's at least show you the numbers.' No fabricated savings figures — the pain of the ongoing bill is the lever.

quote: Yeah. We I mean, we could show you the design over, like, the phone and email if that'd be better for you.
stewart_read: Vincent asks for a quote or contract to be sent. Parker's response describes the appointment delivery format (phone and email review) rather than promising to pre-send a proposal. This is not a codex hard-rule violation — Parker does not promise to email the proposal before the meeting. The genuine risk is customer mental model miscalibration: Vincent may hear 'we can show you over phone and email' as 'they'll send me something to review,' which sets up a no-show when the appointment turns out to be a live call rather than a document delivery. The strategic read — that Parker is using format flexibility to keep a resistant customer engaged — is the more supported hypothesis here.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: Parker caves on the proposal-before-meeting rule. Rejected — Parker does not promise to send anything before the appointment; he describes the appointment itself as a phone/email review session. The rule breach would require a promise to pre-send, which is not present.
coaching_implication: The framing risk is real even without a rule breach. Manager should coach Parker to close the mental model gap explicitly at booking: 'Our specialist will call you on the 21st and walk you through the design live — it's not a document we send ahead, it's a conversation so you can ask questions in real time.' This prevents the no-show that comes from Vincent expecting an email and getting a phone call instead.

quote: Would you mind sending me that to let me text you on a different number. For some reason, the file isn't showing up.
stewart_read: This is the decision point where Parker chooses personal phone over verbal readback. The codex's elderly_low_tech_protocol and landline_protocol both specify: when the primary bill-collection path hits friction, pivot to verbal readback within 2 minutes — not to a secondary personal device. Parker's pivot to personal phone is the anti-pattern the codex flags explicitly: 'rep gives out personal phone number for bill follow-up' is a detection signal for privacy/scale exposure. The verbal readback path (have Vincent read the kWh numbers aloud) was available and unused.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Parker's personal phone pivot is a pragmatic workaround that gets the bill captured and the appointment booked. Partially supported — the outcome is achieved. But the codex flags this as a privacy and scale exposure regardless of outcome, and the verbal readback path would have achieved the same result without the anti-pattern.
coaching_implication: Manager should address two things: (1) the personal phone anti-pattern — Parker should know this creates privacy exposure and is a floor-level policy concern, not just a style choice; (2) the verbal readback alternative — when a file won't come through, 'Can you just read me the kilowatt-hour numbers off the bill?' is faster and cleaner than switching devices. Once Stewart's automated bill capture ships, this becomes the migration trigger.

quote: on my personal phone. It should work better.
stewart_read: Parker executes the personal phone pivot here. The codex's detection signal is explicit: 'rep verbatim contains... personal line... in context of bill collection.' Parker's phrasing ('my personal phone') matches the anti-pattern exactly. The micro-trade framing applies because Parker is substituting a personal commitment (his own phone number) for an institutional process — which the codex identifies as a privacy and scale exposure, not a trust-building move.
alternative_hypothesis: The personal phone pivot could be read as a trust-building micro-trade — giving Vincent a direct line creates intimacy on a cold recycled lead. Rejected — the codex explicitly flags this as an anti-pattern regardless of the trust-building intent, and the verbal readback path achieves bill capture without the exposure.
coaching_implication: Pair this with the 04:14 entry in the 1-on-1. The sequence is: friction at 04:14 → wrong pivot chosen → personal phone executed at 04:24. Coach the decision point, not just the execution. The rep needs to know the verbal readback path exists before they reach the friction moment.

quote: what now we see a a good day to show you that. We can do it over the phone for you.
stewart_read: Parker moves directly from bill receipt to scheduling without completing any verify section beats. The codex requires verify_section as a hard-required beat (call_shape.required_beats, order 2). None of the eight verify items are captured: no address confirmation, no homeowner status, no title/co-owners check, no interest depth, no utility company, no average bill (only the bill photo), no roof type/age, no prior designs. The bill photo substitutes for the bill_collection beat but does not cover the verify section. This is a beat omission, not a knowledge gap.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Parker may be treating this as a recycled lead where prior qualification data is on file, making re-verification redundant. Possible — the codex notes recycled leads may have existing data. But there is no evidence Parker checked for existing records, and the call's opening confusion ('I don't remember I request a information') suggests the lead is cold enough that re-verification would be warranted.
coaching_implication: Manager should walk Parker through the verify section checklist and identify which items were skippable (if prior data exists) vs which were genuinely missed. At minimum: title/co-owners check (spouse protocol) and roof type/age (DQ risk) should have been captured. The closer walks into this appointment without knowing if there's a co-decision-maker or a roof issue.

quote: and we'll just plan to have our specialist give you a call and kinda run through that design.
stewart_read: Parker books the appointment but delivers none of the required button-up elements beyond the date and time. Missing: one-hour block stated, no-driving/at-computer requirement, 'is that fair' handshake, and text-followup promise. The codex marks no_driving_at_computer and is_that_fair_handshake as required, with explicit coaching notes: 'If skipped, no-show rate climbs' and 'Verbal handshake. Predictive of show rate. Don't skip it.' With a two-week gap before the appointment, the no-show risk is elevated.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have kept the close brief to avoid over-complicating a tentative booking with a resistant customer. Partially supported — Vincent's cooperation at this point is real but fragile. However, the button-up anchors exist precisely to protect fragile bookings, not just easy ones. The strategic case for skipping them is weak.
coaching_implication: Manager should drill the full button-up sequence with Parker. The two-week vacation gap makes the 'is that fair' handshake and the computer/no-driving anchor especially important — Vincent needs a clear mental picture of what the 21st appointment actually is before he leaves for vacation. The text followup (with a tieback to the installation-fee concern Parker addressed) would also serve as a re-engagement touchpoint when Vincent returns.
